paragraphs.body: Modern Day Statues and Their Stories

For the longest time humanity has used various forms of art to cherish and immortalize influential figures like famous world leaders, philosophers, and Gods; Paintings, photographs, poems, quotes, biographies, and even music all have been used to memorialize these figures in history. However, of all art forms I feel that nothing quite matches the effect that a statue projects. The symbolism and artistic interpretations possible within a three-dimensional canvas leave so much freedom for an artist to express their creativity in ways unlike any other type of media.

paragraphs.body: In my life, I have been privileged enough to have traveled to a multitude of different countries and experience their cultures firsthand. All the traveling I have done has rooted a deep interest in me to learn about not only the diversity in cultures but the similarities that bring them together. Art from different cultures and times are obviously different; a cave painting from 40,000 years ago is in many ways different from a realistic portrait from modern times, but in a way, they still are connected through the concept that art tells stories.

annotations.comment: this is a potentially interesting idea to explore. What stories has art taught you, specifically?

paragraphs.body: One example from about 30,000 BC is a figurine titled Löwenmensch or Lion Man which was discovered in a cave called Hohlenstein-Stadel located in the German state Baden-Wurttemberg. The figure was carved from mammoth ivory, is about 30cm tall, and depicts a lion’s head on a human body. The sculpture itself is quite simple which in many ways makes it difficult to interpret the meaning, however a popular idea is that the Lion man served as some kind of myth or legend during those times. This idea was supported by the discovery of similar lion-headed sculptures found in caves near Hohlenstein-Stadel. As history progressed, humans evolved, building civilizations and thinking harder than ever before, yet still they continued to produce art that held the same idea as it had for people thousands of years earlier. The beautiful The Löwenmensch marble sculptures of Ancient Greece were delicately detailed and perfected by the virtuosic artists of the time, yet they still serve the purpose of telling a story—in this case, the story of Greek Mythology.

paragraphs.body: In modern times, statues are still created in every country and still exist as a form of art but what do they mean to us now? Do they still hold the same impact as a carved figurine from the stone age or a marble sculpture from Ancient Greece? One recent exploration on the idea was in 2018 after the new world’s tallest statue, titled the Statue of Unity, was revealed in India. In a news article written shortly after the completion titled “A Statue of Unity at a Time of Deep Social, Political Divides,” the author, Viju Cherian argues that the Statue of Unity feels like a gimmick during India’s current Political unrest. He proposes his point by developing several important ideas. First, Cherian points out that the Statue of Unity was unveiled as a symbol of pride for India by citing the celebrations and events associated with its unveiling including “a fly-past by the Indian Air Force, dance, and music performances and a ‘Run for Unity’ orgnised in different cities across the country.” He then counteracts the idea by explaining the decrease in popularity that India’s prime minister, Narendra Modi, has been experiencing since 2013. In the article he provides multiple examples of Modi’s party, the Bharatiya Janata Party, causing civil and political disorder among the people of India. Cherian also expresses his thoughts that even though the statue represents an enormously powerful meaning to India as a country it will not cure its current political situation especially with the protests from local tribes of which land was taken to build the statue on.

annotations.text: Viju Cherian argues that the Statue of Unity feels like a gimmick during India’s current Political unrest.

paragraphs.body: Besides his views on the politics of India, Cherian also comments on the general meaning of statues. He first implies that large statues are not nearly as impactful as they used to be. Many countries now flex feats of engineering as to display their might to the world such as Switzerland which “has the longest rail tunnel” and France which “has the tallest bridge.” Building the world’s tallest statue in the 21st century does not have the same impact as it would have in earlier decades. At the end of his argument, Cherian does hypothesize that the statue could have a positive impact on India with increased tourism and symbolism. However, the people will be the final deciding factor of how it is perceived and in turn preserved over time.

paragraphs.body: After reading Cherian’s article I began to think about how I perceive statues. Whenever I see one, I like to learn about the meaning and what drove the artist to create it. However, as I thought about my own thoughts, I somehow managed to miss the other peoples’ thoughts. Most of the time I would never even consider how the people of the country felt about the statue, I would just assume that no one could ever think a giant statue isn’t cool. Obviously, this is not true at all and can be very clearly seen through the anger towards statues of confederate leaders in the south and Christopher Columbus which are currently being vandalized and torn down. This made me think again about the stories that are emulated by these statues and then again about how perceptions of these stories have changed over time. When I was in elementary school, I was taught that Christopher Columbus discovered North America after sailing the Ocean Blue in 1492. When I got to middle school they taught the story again, except this time in 1492 Christopher Columbus sailed the ocean blue looking for a new path to Asia and discovered not North America, but instead the Bahamas, and when he got there somehow thought he had made it to India. So now Christopher Columbus not only confused himself but confused everyone else into calling the people there Indians instead of Native Americans, which somehow still makes it into APUSH textbooks today. Finally, in high school I learn that Christopher Columbus sailed the Ocean Blue in 1492 looking the middle passage, stumbled upon the Bahamas, met the Natives, started calling them Indians, and then enslaved and slaughtered them. Thinking about this story helped me understand how different people could view these statues based on their understanding or perception of the topic. 4th Grade me would not have understood why people wanted to tear down the statues of the man who discovered our home country. While now, having heard the full story, I can completely understand why. So how does this relate to a modern statue like the Statue of Unity? Statue of Christopher Columbus Covered in Red Paint

paragraphs.body: When I first learned about the Statue of Unity, I began researching the man it portrayed. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was an Indian statesman that was a major figure in the fight for Independence from Britain in the forties. In his life he managed to unify 565 Princely states into one single India which I feel like may have something to do with the naming of his giant statue. From what I learned about him I would see no reason anyone would possibly be angered by the statue, he seemed like an important figure in India even before it was India. However, I am not Indian, and I’ve never been to India. So, what do the people of India think about the Statue of Unity?

annotations.text: Indian, and I’ve never been to India.

paragraphs.body: In Viju Cherian’s article, “A Statue of Unity at a time of Deep Social, Political Divides,” he writes, “Divisions across political ideologies, on religious and caste identities and on economic lines are unfortunately evident today, and, like never before, they are threatening the unity of India — the India which Sardar Patel Image of The Statue of Unity at Night amalgamated by bringing together 500-plus princely states. The Statue of Unity is unlikely to bridge these divides.” In this quote, Cherian is basically saying, India is in a time of discord and the precious unity that Sardar Patel fought for is not going to be saved by some 600ft tall statue no matter how much symbolism and meaning is stuffed into it. Cherian obviously praises Sardar Patel, even mentioning his amazing accomplishment of unity, but what good is his likeness if his hard work is gone? It would still hold meaning but the whole tone would change. A 600ft statue of Patel watching his life’s work fade into history as the people of India view the artwork as a monument of what was. The statue does not lack important symbolism, but it doesn’t create change, and I think that is the point Cherian and the people of India are trying to express.

annotations.text: but it doesn’t create change, and I think that is the point Cherian and the people of India are trying to express.

paragraphs.body: In another quote from Viju Cherian’s article he argues, “It is in such a situation that many are asking the rationale behind a Rs 2,989-crore statue. The symbolism behind tall, imposing statues is not a new one — in fact, it is an old one which lacks the awe and aura it once had.” The essence of Cherian’s argument in this quote is one of Indias’ distaste for the decision to spend almost $400 million on a statue instead of addressing the current events. Cherian then adds his opinion on large statues, knocking the ideology and marking it as an “old one which lacks the awe and aura it once had.” In my opinion, his assessment is a little harsh, I believe that the statue still holds this amazing awe factor purely based on the immense size. However, I do agree that statues don’t hold as much meaning to us now in a modern age as they once did.

paragraphs.body: A long time ago, statues held a lot more meaning through religion than they do now. A big example of religion in statues is that of Buddhist depictions. Statues of Buddhist deities today are definitely still prevalent, if you go anywhere in China, you will see thousands of little figurines of Buddha and Buddhist deities. But, thousands of The Larger Statue of Vairocana before destruction The Smaller Statue of Sakyamuni before destruction years ago, ginormous carvings of Buddhas could be found everywhere around the world in various rockfaces. One of my favorites is the Buddhas of Bamiyan which had depicted Vairocana and Sakyamuni, two prominent Buddhas. The two were thought to have been carved around the 6th Century making them more than a thousand years old. Historians who studied the statues believe that they were used as holy sites for merchants traveling along the silk road, and in general it was thought that the engravings were created to provide protection over them. What these statues meant to those traveling merchants pales in comparison to what statues represent today.

annotations.comment: Again, interesting information, but is the essay returning to "report" rather than developing one single central focusing idea?

paragraphs.body: Between the Buddhas of Bamiyan, the Statue of Unity¸ the Ancient Greek Sculptures of Gods, and the little Löwenmensch, there is still a story to be told for each of them. All though the Buddhas of Bamiyan were destroyed in 2001 during the Afghan Civil War, their stories are still remembered today which I think goes to show that even if these statues do not hold the power to shift political turmoil or end wars, they still hold a deep and unique meaning to the people.
